perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, Proprietary Information shall mean, without limitation, computer programs, code, algorithms, names and expertise of employees and consultants, formulas, processes, inventions, schematics and other technical, business, financial, pricing and product development plans, forecasts and strategies, together with any analysis or tangible embodiments of the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information created by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, Proprietary Information shall mean computer programs, code, algorithms, names and expertise of employees and consultants, formulas, processes, inventions, schematics and other technical, business, financial, pricing and product development plans, forecasts and strategies. Any analysis or tangible embodiments are explicitly excluded from the definition of Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition explicitly excludes 'any analysis or tangible embodiments' from being considered Proprietary Information, directly contradicting the original definition which includes them.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines a trade secret to include a broad range of information, including analyses and compilations if they derive independent economic value. By excluding analyses and tangible embodiments, the modified definition narrows the scope of 'Proprietary Information' to such an extent that some items that would be considered trade secrets under Connecticut law are no longer protected by the contract.

Previous Next As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means, including searching through trash. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that i
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the law by explicitly excluding 'any analysis or tangible embodiments' from the definition of Proprietary Information. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA (Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act) defines a trade secret to include analyses and compilations if they derive independent economic value. The modified text narrows the definition of 'Proprietary Information' so much that items considered trade secrets under Connecticut law are no longer protected by the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Connecticut General Statutes § 35-51, defines misappropriation to include the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means or disclosure/use of a trade secret without consent, highlighting the importance of protecting trade secrets. The original contract included analyses and tangible embodiments, aligning with the broad scope of trade secrets protected under CUTSA. The change directly contradicts the intent and scope of trade secret protection as defined by Connecticut law, making the contract less compliant with CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall preserve Proprietary Information received from the Disclosing Party in confidence by exercising at least the same degree of care used to restrict disclosure and use of the Receiving Party's own similar confidential information, but at least a reasonable degree of care, and shall refrain from disclosing such Propriety Information to its employees not involved in the Pilot Study, its consultants, representatives, or agents ( the "Affiliates") or to any third party without written authorization from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall preserve Proprietary Information received from the Disclosing Party in confidence by exercising commercially reasonable efforts, and shall refrain from disclosing such Propriety Information to its employees not involved in the Pilot Study, its consultants, representatives, or agents ( the "Affiliates") or to any third party without written authorization from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "at least the same degree of care used to restrict disclosure and use of the Receiving Party's own similar confidential information, but at least a reasonable degree of care" has been replaced with the looser standard "commercially reasonable efforts."
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA implies a higher standard of care for protecting trade secrets than "commercially reasonable efforts." The change weakens the protection of proprietary information below what would be required to maintain trade secret status under Connecticut law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language requires the Receiving Party to protect Proprietary Information with "at least the same degree of care used to restrict disclosure and use of the Receiving Party's own similar confidential information, but at least a reasonable degree of care". The modified text replaces this with "commercially reasonable efforts." The Law Explanation states that CUTSA (Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act) implies a higher standard of care than "commercially reasonable efforts" for trade secret protection, and that the change weakens the protection below what would be required to maintain trade secret status under Connecticut law. Thus, the change contradicts the standard of care required to maintain trade secret protection, as "commercially reasonable efforts" is a lower standard.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Connecticut and shall be enforceable in the courts of that state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles, and shall be enforceable in the courts of that state.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law is changed from Connecticut to Delaware and specifies the conflict of laws principles shall be ignored. The agreement involves Yale University School of Medicine in Connecticut, so changing the jurisdiction to Delaware impacts which state's trade secret laws will be applied.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, Connecticut courts often consider the location of the parties and the subject matter of the contract. Switching to Delaware law, especially without considering conflict of law principles, could be challenged if the contract's primary connections are with Connecticut, making the choice of law provision potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The contract was modified to change the governing law from Connecticut to Delaware, while the agreement involves Yale University School of Medicine in Connecticut. The Law Explanation indicates that Connecticut courts often consider the location of the parties and subject matter; switching to Delaware law could be challenged if the contract's primary connections are with Connecticut. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions connected to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law is used. The change in governing law, especially without considering conflict of laws principles, creates a potential legal contradiction because it disregards the established connection to Connecticut and could lead to the application of a different state's laws inappropriately, thus deviating from established conflict of law principles.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that in the event of any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information without the prior express written consent of the Disclosing Party, the Disclosing Party will suffer an irreparable injury, such that no remedy at law will afford the Disclosing Party with adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, such injury. The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Disclosing Party may be entitled to specific performance of the Receiving Party's obligations under this Agreement, as well as further relief (injunctive or otherwise) as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that in the event of any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information without the prior express written consent of the Disclosing Party, monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy available to the Disclosing Party, and the Disclosing Party explicitly waives any right to seek injunctive relief or specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: The original text assumes irreparable injury and allows for specific performance/injunctive relief. The modified text explicitly states that only monetary damages are available and waives any right to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By contractually waiving the right to seek injunctive relief, the agreement limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in a way that is inconsistent with the protections offered by the statute. A Connecticut court may not enforce this waiver if it finds that it undermines the purpose of CUTSA.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly waives the right to seek injunctive relief or specific performance, limiting the remedies available to the Disclosing Party to monetary damages only. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By waiving this right, the agreement limits the remedies in a way inconsistent with the statute's protections. This creates a legal contradiction because the contract actively restricts a remedy that the law makes available, falling under the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.
